eli3: Conditional probability is the probability of A given that B has occurred: P(A|B). It updates our beliefs based on new information. If B happened, we're in a smaller, more specific world.
Formula: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). We restrict to outcomes where B occurred, then ask what fraction also have A. The sample space shrinks from Ω to B.

Conditional probability is the foundation of Bayesian inference, medical testing (what's the chance of disease given positive test?), and sequential decision making (updating beliefs as we observe data).

whenWhy.title: Bayesian inference and updating
whenWhy.body: Start with prior P(θ), observe data D, compute posterior P(θ|D) ∝ P(D|θ)P(θ). All Bayesian statistics is built on conditional probability. Enables principled belief updating.

whenWhy.title: Medical testing and diagnosis
whenWhy.body: Positive test result: P(Disease|Positive) = P(Positive|Disease)P(Disease)/P(Positive). Even with accurate tests, if disease is rare, most positives are false positives. Conditional probability prevents base rate fallacy.

whenWhy.title: Markov chains and sequential models
whenWhy.body: Markov property: future state depends only on current state, not full history. P(X_{t+1}|X_t, X_{t-1},…) = P(X_{t+1}|X_t). Conditional independence simplifies modeling of sequences (NLP, time series, reinforcement learning).

whenWhy.title: A/B testing and causal inference
whenWhy.body: Condition on treatment assignment to estimate causal effects. P(Outcome|Treatment) vs P(Outcome|Control). Randomization ensures conditioning is valid; observational studies require adjusting for confounders.

deepDiveIntro: For events A, B with P(B) > 0, the conditional probability of A given B is P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B). This defines a new probability measure on the restricted space where B occurred. Intuitively, we 'zoom in' to outcomes consistent with B.

Independence means P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B), equivalently P(A|B) = P(A) — knowing B doesn't change A's probability. Conditional independence generalizes: P(A ∩ B|C) = P(A|C)P(B|C). Chain rule: P(A₁ ∩ … ∩ Aₙ) = P(A₁)P(A₂|A₁)P(A₃|A₁∩A₂)…

deepDive.heading: Definition and Intuition
deepDive.body: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) for P(B) > 0. Geometrically: shrink sample space to B, measure A's share. If A and B are independent, P(A|B) = P(A). Conditioning always increases some probabilities and decreases others relative to unconditional.

deepDive.heading: Law of Total Probability
deepDive.body: If {Bᵢ} partitions Ω: P(A) = Σᵢ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ). Allows computing unconditional probability by conditioning on cases. Essential when direct computation of P(A) is hard but conditional probabilities are known. Bayes' theorem inverts this.

deepDive.heading: Chain Rule (Product Rule)
deepDive.body: P(A ∩ B) = P(A|B)P(B) = P(B|A)P(A). For multiple events: P(A₁ ∩ … ∩ Aₙ) = P(A₁)P(A₂|A₁)P(A₃|A₁∩A₂)…P(Aₙ|A₁∩…∩Aₙ₋₁). Factorizes joint distributions into conditionals — basis for probabilistic graphical models.

deepDive.heading: Independence and Conditional Independence
deepDive.body: A ⟂ B: P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B). A ⟂ B | C: P(A ∩ B|C) = P(A|C)P(B|C). Conditional independence doesn't imply unconditional independence, and vice versa. Key structure in Bayesian networks, Markov random fields, and latent variable models.

deepDive.heading: Bayes' Theorem
deepDive.body: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B). Inverts conditioning: from P(effect|cause) to P(cause|effect). Prior P(A) updated by likelihood P(B|A) to give posterior P(A|B). Denominator P(B) = Σ P(B|Aᵢ)P(Aᵢ) normalizes.

takeaways: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B): probability of A given B occurred.
Independence: P(A|B) = P(A); conditional independence given C.
Law of total probability: P(A) = Σ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ).

edgeCases.a: Conditional probability P(A|B) is undefined when P(B) = 0. For continuous random variables, we use limits: P(A|X=x) requires conditioning on X ∈ [x-ε, x+ε] and taking ε→0, leading to conditional density functions.

edgeCases.q: Can events be independent but conditionally dependent?
edgeCases.a: Yes. Example: A = coin 1 heads, B = coin 2 heads — independent. But given C = 'total heads = 1', A and B are dependent: if A occurred, B definitely didn't. Conversely, events can be dependent but conditionally independent given a third variable (explaining away).

A trend appears in several groups of data but disappears or reverses when groups are combined. Arises from conditioning on confounding variables. Example: Treatment appears effective in both subgroups but harmful overall due to different baseline risks.

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 1
Kolmogorov — Foundations of the Theory of Probability
